name: Masonic Handshake
description: The Masonic handshake, also known as the 'grip,' is a series of secret handshakes used by Freemasons to identify themselves to one another. Proponents claim these handshakes are ancient, dating back to the operative masons who built medieval cathedrals, and that they serve as a means of verifying Masonic membership. The specific grips vary by degree within Freemasonry, with each degree having its own distinct handshake. Outside of Freemasonry, the existence and supposed details of these handshakes have become fodder for conspiracy theories. These theories often allege that the handshakes are used by Masons in positions of power to subtly signal their allegiance and influence events behind the scenes. Some theorists interpret historical figures shaking hands in portraits or photographs as evidence of Masonic affiliation and conspiratorial activity. Mainstream scholarship views the Masonic handshake as a relatively modern development within Freemasonry, originating in the 18th century. Historians point to the lack of documentary evidence for secret handshakes predating this period. They argue that the handshakes serve primarily as a symbolic ritual within the Masonic fraternity, fostering camaraderie and reinforcing the organization's hierarchical structure. The secrecy surrounding the handshakes is understood as a means of maintaining the mystique and exclusivity of the organization, rather than as a tool for clandestine control.
subtitle: Secret grips of Freemasonry.

claims.claim: Masonic handshakes are ancient, dating back to operative masons.
claims.evidence: Oral tradition within Freemasonry; interpretations of medieval art.
claims.criticism: Lack of documentary evidence predating the 18th century; historical accounts suggest a later origin.

claims.claim: Masonic handshakes are used by Masons in positions of power to identify each other and exert influence.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of handshakes in historical photographs and portraits; anecdotal accounts.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence of coordinated action based on handshakes; interpretations are often speculative and lack context.

claims.claim: Specific Masonic handshakes indicate the degree of membership within the organization.
claims.evidence: Descriptions of different handshakes in Masonic literature and revealed by former members.
claims.criticism: While different grips exist for different degrees, this claim is more about internal structure than a conspiracy. Public revelation of grips doesn't inherently prove external influence.

keyFigures.name: Albert Mackey
keyFigures.role: Author of Masonic encyclopedias and historian of Freemasonry.

keyFigures.name: Jasper Ridley
keyFigures.role: Historian who examined Freemasonry's influence, but also debunked many conspiracy theories about it.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to initiation rituals in ancient mystery schools

timeline.event: Emergence of documented Masonic rituals, including handshakes.
Spread of Freemasonry globally and increased speculation about its influence.

sources.author: Jasper Ridley
sources.work: The Freemasons: A History of the World's Most Powerful Secret Society
sources.title: Jasper Ridley

sources.author: John J. Robinson
sources.work: Born in Blood: The Lost Secrets of Freemasonry
sources.title: John J. Robinson
